sections.title: Listening
sections.description: 40 questions • Approximately 30 minutes • You will hear each recording only once

parts.title: Part 1: Conversation about joining a sports club
parts.instructions: Listen to the conversation and answer questions 1-10.

script.narrator:
You will hear a conversation between a sports club manager and a man who wants to join the club. First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 10.

[Pause]

Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 10.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Good morning, City Sports Club. How can I help you?

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Hello, I'm interested in joining the club. Could you tell me about the membership options?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Certainly. We have three main types of membership. The basic membership is £35 per month, which gives you access to the gym and swimming pool.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: What about classes? I'm particularly interested in yoga and tennis.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: For classes, you'd need our premium membership at £50 per month. That includes all group classes and court bookings.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Is there a student discount available?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Yes, students get 20% off all memberships. You'll need to show your student ID when you register.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Great. What are your opening hours?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: We're open from 6am to 10pm on weekdays, and 8am to 8pm on weekends. The swimming pool closes one hour earlier for cleaning.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Do you have parking facilities?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Yes, we have a car park with 150 spaces. It's free for members for up to three hours.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: What about public transport? I don't have a car.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: The number 25 bus stops right outside, and the nearest train station is about 10 minutes walk away.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Can I try the facilities before joining?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Of course. We offer a free one-day trial pass. You can use all the facilities and even join one group class.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: That sounds perfect. How do I arrange that?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Just come to reception with some ID, and we'll set it up for you. What's your name?

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: It's David Chen.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Okay David, I'll make a note. We look forward to seeing you.

questions.questionText: How much is the basic membership?
Which membership includes group classes?
What discount do students receive?
When does the club close on weekdays?
What closes one hour earlier?
How many parking spaces are available?
Which bus stops outside the club?
What is offered for free?
What is needed for the trial?
What is the man's name?

parts.title: Part 2: Talk about a museum exhibition
parts.instructions: Listen to the talk and answer questions 11-20.

script.narrator:
Now turn to Part 2. You will hear a guide talking about a museum exhibition. Look at questions 11 to 20.

[Pause]

Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 20.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Good afternoon everyone and welcome to the City Museum. I'm here to tell you about our special exhibition 'Ancient Civilizations' which runs from November until March next year. This exhibition features artifacts from three ancient cultures: Egypt, Rome, and China. Let me start with the Egyptian section, which includes real mummies, jewellery, and pottery dating back over 3,000 years. The Roman section displays coins, weapons, and household items from the Roman Empire. Finally, the Chinese section shows beautiful silk textiles, bronze vessels, and early porcelain. The exhibition is open daily from 9am to 5pm, with extended hours until 7pm on Fridays. Admission is £12 for adults, £8 for students and seniors, and free for children under 12. We recommend allowing at least two hours to see everything properly. Guided tours are available at 11am and 2pm daily, and these are included in the admission price. There's also an audio guide available in five languages: English, Spanish, French, German, and Mandarin. The museum shop has replica artifacts and books about ancient history. Photography is allowed but without flash. The nearest car park is on Queen Street, about five minutes walk away. Alternatively, the museum is easily reached by bus or underground. The nearest tube station is Museum Station on the Central Line. Are there any questions before we begin our tour?

questions.questionText: How long does the exhibition run?
Which ancient civilization is NOT mentioned?
What does the Egyptian section include?
When are extended opening hours?
How much is student admission?
How long does the guide recommend for visiting?
What languages are available for audio guides?
Where is the nearest car park?
Which tube line serves Museum Station?
What is restricted in photography?

parts.title: Part 3: Discussion between students about a presentation
parts.instructions: Listen to the discussion and answer questions 21-30.

script.narrator:
Now turn to Part 3. You will hear two students discussing a group presentation. Look at questions 21 to 30.

[Pause]

Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 30.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Hi James, I'm worried about our history presentation next week. Have you started preparing yet?

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: I've done some research, Sarah. Our topic is the Industrial Revolution, right? I found some good information about the textile industry.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Yes, that's correct. I was thinking we should focus on three main areas: technological changes, social impacts, and economic effects.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: That sounds good. How about we divide the research? I can cover the technological changes and you research the social impacts. Then we can both work on the economic effects.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Okay, that works for me. When should we meet to combine our research?

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: How about Thursday afternoon? That gives us two days to do individual research first.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Thursday is good for me. Should we meet in the library or the student common room?

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Let's meet in the library study room. It's quieter there. I'll book room 3B for 2pm.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Perfect. What about visual aids for the presentation? Do you think we should use PowerPoint or posters?

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: I think PowerPoint would be better. We can include historical images and graphs. I'm good with PowerPoint, so I can create the slides.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Great. How long does the presentation need to be?

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Dr. Brown said 15 minutes, plus 5 minutes for questions. So we should prepare about 12-13 slides.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Should we practice together before the actual presentation?

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Definitely. How about we practice on Monday afternoon? That gives us time to make changes if needed.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Monday works for me. Where should we practice?

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: We can use the empty classroom on the second floor. I'll check if it's available.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Okay, thanks James. I feel better now that we have a plan.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: No problem. Don't worry, we'll do fine. Just make sure to bring your notes on Thursday.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: I will. See you then!

questions.questionText: What subject is the presentation for?
What is the specific topic?
What three areas will they cover?
What part will James research?
When will they meet to combine research?
Where will they meet?
What visual aid will they use?
How long should the presentation be?
When will they practice?
Where will they practice?

parts.title: Part 4: Lecture on climate change
parts.instructions: Listen to the lecture and answer questions 31-40.

script.narrator:
Now turn to Part 4. You will hear a lecture on climate change. Look at questions 31 to 40.

[Pause]

Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Good morning. Today we're going to discuss the main causes and effects of climate change. Climate change refers to long-term shifts in temperatures and weather patterns. While these shifts can be natural, since the 1800s, human activities have been the main driver of climate change. The primary cause is the burning of fossil fuels like coal, oil, and gas. This produces greenhouse gases that trap heat in the atmosphere. The main greenhouse gas is carbon dioxide, but methane and nitrous oxide are also significant contributors. Deforestation is another major cause, as trees absorb carbon dioxide and their removal increases atmospheric CO2 levels. The effects of climate change are widespread. We're seeing rising sea levels due to melting ice caps and glaciers. Extreme weather events like hurricanes and droughts are becoming more frequent and intense. Agriculture is affected through changes in growing seasons and water availability. Biodiversity is threatened as species struggle to adapt to changing conditions. To address climate change, we need to transition to renewable energy sources like solar and wind power. Energy efficiency improvements and changes in transportation systems are also crucial. International cooperation is essential, as shown by agreements like the Paris Agreement. Individuals can contribute by reducing energy consumption, using public transport, and supporting sustainable products. In our next lecture, we'll examine specific climate change solutions in more detail.

questions.questionText: What is the main topic of the lecture?
Since when have humans been the main cause of climate change?
What is the primary cause mentioned?
What is the main greenhouse gas?
What is mentioned as another major cause?
Why are sea levels rising?
What extreme weather events are mentioned?
What renewable energy sources are suggested?
What international agreement is mentioned?
What will the next lecture cover?

passages.title: Passage 1: The Benefits of Cycling
passages.content:
Cycling is becoming increasingly popular in cities around the world. Many people are choosing bicycles as their main mode of transport for several reasons.

One major benefit of cycling is improved health. Regular cycling strengthens muscles, improves cardiovascular fitness, and helps maintain a healthy weight. It's a low-impact exercise that's suitable for people of all ages. Studies show that people who cycle regularly have lower risks of heart disease, diabetes, and some types of cancer.

Cycling is also good for the environment. Unlike cars, bicycles don't produce air pollution or greenhouse gases. They require much less energy to manufacture than cars and don't need fossil fuels to operate. By choosing to cycle instead of drive, individuals can significantly reduce their carbon footprint.

From an economic perspective, cycling is much cheaper than driving a car or using public transport. The initial cost of a bicycle is relatively low, and maintenance costs are minimal. Cyclists don't need to pay for fuel, insurance, or parking fees. Many employers now offer cycle-to-work schemes that provide tax benefits for people who commute by bicycle.

Urban planning has begun to reflect the growing popularity of cycling. Many cities are investing in cycle lanes, secure parking facilities, and bike-sharing programs. These improvements make cycling safer and more convenient, encouraging even more people to take up cycling.

Despite these benefits, some challenges remain. Safety concerns deter many potential cyclists, especially in cities with heavy traffic and inadequate cycling infrastructure. Weather conditions can also be a barrier in some regions. However, with proper safety equipment and clothing, these challenges can be overcome.

Overall, the growth of cycling represents a positive trend for public health, the environment, and urban living. As cities continue to develop cycling infrastructure, this trend is likely to continue.

questions.questionText: What is the main topic of the passage?
What health benefit is NOT mentioned?
Why is cycling good for the environment?
What economic advantage is mentioned?
What are cities investing in?
What deters potential cyclists?
What can help overcome weather challenges?
What do employers offer?
What is the author's overall view?
What trend is likely to continue?

passages.title: Passage 2: The History of Coffee
passages.content:
Coffee is one of the most popular beverages in the world, with a history dating back centuries. The origins of coffee are believed to be in Ethiopia, where legend says a goat herder discovered the energizing effects of coffee beans.

From Ethiopia, coffee spread to the Arabian Peninsula. By the 15th century, coffee was being grown in Yemen and traded throughout the Middle East. Coffee houses became important social centers where people gathered to discuss politics, business, and culture. These establishments were sometimes called 'schools of the wise' because of the intellectual conversations that took place there.

Coffee reached Europe in the 17th century through trade with the Ottoman Empire. Initially, there was some opposition from religious leaders who called it the 'bitter invention of Satan.' However, after Pope Clement VIII tasted coffee and approved it, its popularity grew rapidly. The first European coffee house opened in Venice in 1645.

In the 18th century, coffee cultivation spread to the Americas. The French brought coffee plants to Martinique, and from there, coffee growing expanded throughout Central and South America. Brazil eventually became the world's largest coffee producer, a position it still holds today.

The industrial revolution brought significant changes to coffee production and consumption. Instant coffee was invented in the early 20th century, making preparation quicker and easier. The invention of vacuum packaging helped preserve freshness, allowing coffee to be transported over long distances.

Today, coffee is a global industry worth billions of dollars. Specialty coffee shops have become ubiquitous in cities worldwide. There's growing interest in sustainable and fair trade coffee, which ensures better conditions for coffee farmers.

Despite its long history, coffee continues to evolve. New brewing methods and coffee varieties are constantly being developed. The story of coffee demonstrates how a simple beverage can have a profound impact on global culture and economy.

questions.questionText: Where did coffee originate?
What were coffee houses called?
How did coffee reach Europe?
Who approved coffee for Christians?
Where was the first European coffee house?
Which country is the largest coffee producer?
What was invented in the 20th century?
What helped preserve coffee during transport?
What type of coffee is growing in popularity?
What does coffee's story demonstrate?

passages.title: Passage 3: The Importance of Sleep
passages.content:
Sleep is essential for good health and well-being. Despite this, many people don't get enough quality sleep. Understanding the importance of sleep can help people prioritize this vital activity.

Sleep plays a crucial role in physical health. During sleep, the body repairs tissues, builds bone and muscle, and strengthens the immune system. Lack of sleep has been linked to various health problems, including obesity, heart disease, and diabetes. Adults typically need 7-9 hours of sleep per night for optimal health.

Mental health is also closely connected to sleep. Sleep helps process emotions and consolidate memories. People who get adequate sleep tend to have better concentration, problem-solving skills, and creativity. Chronic sleep deprivation can contribute to anxiety, depression, and other mental health issues.

There are several stages of sleep, each with different functions. Light sleep helps with memory and learning. Deep sleep is important for physical restoration. REM (Rapid Eye Movement) sleep, when most dreaming occurs, is crucial for emotional regulation and creative thinking.

Modern lifestyle factors often interfere with sleep. The use of electronic devices before bed can disrupt natural sleep patterns because of the blue light they emit. Irregular work schedules, stress, and poor sleep environments also contribute to sleep problems.

Improving sleep quality involves developing good sleep habits. Maintaining a consistent sleep schedule, even on weekends, helps regulate the body's internal clock. Creating a comfortable sleep environment - cool, dark, and quiet - promotes better sleep. Avoiding caffeine and heavy meals before bedtime can also help.

For those with persistent sleep problems, professional help may be necessary. Sleep disorders like insomnia and sleep apnea require medical treatment. However, for most people, simple lifestyle changes can significantly improve sleep quality.

Prioritizing sleep is one of the most effective ways to improve overall health and quality of life. As research continues to reveal the extensive benefits of sleep, its importance becomes increasingly clear.

questions.questionText: What is the main topic of the passage?
How much sleep do adults typically need?
What physical health problem is linked to lack of sleep?
What mental benefit comes from adequate sleep?
Which sleep stage is for physical restoration?
What disrupts natural sleep patterns?
What helps regulate the body clock?
What should be avoided before bedtime?
What requires medical treatment?
What is one of the most effective health improvements?

tasks.title: Writing Task 1
tasks.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on this task. The table below shows the percentage of students choosing different university subjects in a European country in 1990 and 2020. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.
tasks.description: You will be presented with a table showing student subject choices over time.

sampleData.title: University Subject Choices (1990 vs 2020)
sampleData.description: Table showing changes in percentage of students choosing different university subjects between 1990 and 2020

tasks.title: Writing Task 2
tasks.instructions: You should spend about 40 minutes on this task. Some people think that children should start learning a foreign language at primary school, while others believe it is better to wait until secondary school. Discuss both views and give your own opinion. Write at least 250 words.
tasks.description: You will be presented with contrasting views on language learning age and asked to discuss them.
keyPoints: Arguments for starting foreign languages early
Arguments for waiting until secondary school

parts.title: Part 1: Verb Tenses and Forms
parts.instructions: Choose the correct verb form to complete each sentence.

questions.questionText: If I ______ more time, I would travel more.
She has lived in London ______ ten years.
By this time next year, I ______ university.
When I arrived, they ______ already ______ dinner.
I look forward to ______ from you soon.
The number of students in the class ______ increased this year.
If it ______ tomorrow, we'll cancel the picnic.
He ______ to the gym every day last week.
By the time she arrives, we ______ waiting for two hours.
I wish I ______ speak French fluently.

parts.title: Part 2: Prepositions and Articles
parts.instructions: Choose the correct preposition or article to complete each sentence.

questions.questionText: I'm interested ______ learning more about this topic.
She arrived ______ the airport just in time.
Could you pass me ______ salt, please?
He's been working here ______ 2015.
I'm not very good ______ mathematics.
We're looking forward ______ our holiday.
She was born ______ a small village.
I'll see you ______ Monday morning.
He apologized ______ being late.
This is ______ interesting book.

parts.title: Part 3: Sentence Structure and Word Order
parts.instructions: Choose the sentence with the correct word order and structure.

questions.questionText: Which sentence is correct?
options: A) She always is late for meetings.
B) She is late always for meetings.
C) She is always late for meetings.
D) Always she is late for meetings.

questions.questionText: Which sentence is correct?
options: A) I yesterday went to the cinema.
B) I went to the cinema yesterday.
C) I went yesterday to the cinema.
D) Yesterday I went to the cinema.

questions.questionText: Which sentence is correct?
options: A) He can speak very well English.
B) He can speak English very well.
C) He can very well speak English.
D) Very well he can speak English.

questions.questionText: Which question is correct?
options: A) You like do Chinese food?
B) Do you like Chinese food?
D) You do like Chinese food?

questions.questionText: Which sentence is correct?
options: A) They have often dinner at 7pm.
B) They often have dinner at 7pm.
C) Often they have dinner at 7pm.
D) They have dinner often at 7pm.

questions.questionText: Which sentence is correct?
options: A) I'm not understanding the question.
B) I don't understand the question.
C) I'm not understand the question.
D) I don't understanding the question.

questions.questionText: Which sentence is correct?
options: A) She told me that she will come tomorrow.
B) She told me that she would come tomorrow.
C) She told me that she comes tomorrow.
D) She told me that she came tomorrow.

questions.questionText: Which sentence is correct?
options: A) The book who I read was interesting.
B) The book that I read was interesting.
C) The book what I read was interesting.
D) The book which I read it was interesting.

questions.questionText: Which sentence is correct?
options: A) Despite it was raining, we went out.
B) Despite the rain, we went out.
C) Despite of the rain, we went out.
D) Despite raining, we went out.

questions.questionText: Which sentence is correct?
options: A) I have visited Paris last year.
B) I visited Paris last year.
C) I was visiting Paris last year.
D) I had visited Paris last year.

parts.title: Part 4: Vocabulary and Word Choice
parts.instructions: Choose the word or phrase that best completes each sentence.

questions.questionText: The government should invest ______ in public transport.
It was a ______ difficult exam, but I passed.
The company made a ______ last year.
She speaks English ______.
We need to find a ______ to this problem.
The weather was ______, so we stayed inside.
He's ______ in science and technology.
The movie was ______ boring that I fell asleep.
We had a ______ discussion about the environment.
The results were ______ better than we expected.

metadata.author: IELTS Academic Testing System
metadata.notes: IELTS Academic Test for 26 November 2025 with realistic questions and common topics. This test includes typical IELTS themes like health, environment, education, and daily life, suitable for band 5.0 candidates. The speaking section has been replaced with grammar to focus on the user's identified weakness.
